title: Charmed Life - Chapter 5 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter Five

5

G
 WENDOLEN REFUSED to tell Cat what she was going to do. This meant that Cat had rather a melancholy time. After a wholesome lunch of turnips and boiled mutton, they had lessons again. After that, Gwendolen ran hastily away and would not let Cat come with her. Cat did not know what to do.

‚ÄúWould you care to come out and play?‚ÄĚ Roger asked.

Cat looked at him and saw that he was just being polite. ‚ÄúNo, thank you,‚ÄĚ he said politely. He was forced to wander around the gardens on his own. There was a wood lower down, full of horse chestnuts, but they were not nearly ripe. As Cat was halfheartedly staring up into one, he saw there was a tree house in it, about halfway up. This was more like it. Cat was just about to climb up to it when he heard voices and saw Julia‚Äôs skirt flutter among the leaves. So that was no good. It was Julia and Roger‚Äôs private tree house, and they were in it.

Cat wandered away again. He came to the lawn, and there was Gwendolen, crouching under one of the cedars, very busy digging a small hole.

‚ÄúWhat are you doing?‚ÄĚ said Cat.

‚ÄúGo away,‚ÄĚ said Gwendolen.

Cat went away. He was sure what Gwendolen was doing was witchcraft and had to do with teaching Chrestomanci a lesson, but it was no good asking Gwendolen when she was being this secretive. Cat had to wait. He waited through another terrifying dinner, and then through a long, long evening. Gwendolen locked herself in her room after dinner and told him to go away when he knocked.

Next morning, Cat woke up early and hurried to the nearest of his three windows. He saw at once what Gwendolen had been doing. The lawn was ruined. It was not a smooth stretch of green velvet any longer. It was a mass of molehills. As far as Cat could see in both directions, there were little green mounds, little heaps of raw earth, long lines of raw earth and long green furrows of raised grass. There must have been an army of moles at work on it all night. About a dozen gardeners were standing in a gloomy huddle, scratching their heads over it.

Cat threw on his clothes and dashed downstairs.

Gwendolen was leaning out of her window in her frilly cotton nightdress, glowing with pride. ‚ÄúLook at that!‚ÄĚ she said to Cat. ‚ÄúIsn‚Äôt it marvelous! There‚Äôs acres of it too. It took me hours yesterday evening to make sure it was all spoiled. That will make Chrestomanci think a bit!‚ÄĚ

Cat was sure it would. He did not know how much a huge stretch of turf like that would cost to replace, but he suspected it was a great deal. He was afraid Gwendolen would be in really bad trouble.

But, to his astonishment, nobody so much as mentioned the lawn. Euphemia came in a minute later, but all she said was, ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll both be late for your breakfast again.‚ÄĚ Roger and Julia said nothing at all. They silently accepted the marmalade and Cat‚Äôs knife when he passed them over, but the sole thing either of them said was when Julia dropped Cat‚Äôs knife and picked it up again, all fluffy. She said, ‚ÄúBother!‚ÄĚ And when Mr. Saunders called them through for lessons, the only things he talked about were what he was teaching them. Cat decided that nobody knew Gwendolen had caused the moles. They could have no idea what a strong witch she was.

There were no lessons after lunch that day. Mr. Saunders explained that they always had Wednesday afternoons off. And at lunchtime, every molehill had gone. When they looked out of the playroom window, the lawn was like a sheet of velvet again.

vocabulary.definition: Very surprised or amazed.
vocabulary.example: Cat was afraid Gwendolen would be in really bad trouble, but was astonished nobody mentioned the lawn.

vocabulary.definition: Great surprise or amazement.
vocabulary.example: But, to his astonishment, nobody mentioned the lawn.

vocabulary.definition: Expresses annoyance, frustration, or mild anger.
vocabulary.example: Julia said, ‚ÄúBother!‚ÄĚ

vocabulary.definition: Adopting a position with knees bent and body lowered.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen, crouching under one of the cedars.

vocabulary.definition: Proper name, important as a character in the narrative.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia came in a minute later.

vocabulary.definition: Clearly seen or understood; obvious.
vocabulary.example: The lawn was ruined. It was not a smooth stretch of green velvet any longer.

vocabulary.definition: Covered with soft light hair or down.
vocabulary.example: Julia dropped Cat‚Äôs knife and picked it up again, all fluffy.

vocabulary.definition: To wave or flap rapidly and lightly.
vocabulary.example: Julia‚Äôs skirt flutter among the leaves.

vocabulary.definition: A long narrow trench made in the ground by a plough, especially for planting seeds or irrigation.
vocabulary.example: Long green furrows of raised grass.

vocabulary.definition: Dark or poorly lit, especially to appear depressing or frightening.
vocabulary.example: A gloomy huddle.

vocabulary.definition: Expressing great praise or enthusiasm; also physically shining brightly.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was glowing with pride.

vocabulary.definition: Without enthusiasm or energy; showing little interest or effort.
vocabulary.example: Cat was halfheartedly staring up into one.

vocabulary.definition: In a hurried or rushed manner.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen ran hastily away.

vocabulary.definition: A closely packed group of people or things.
vocabulary.example: About a dozen gardeners were standing in a gloomy huddle.

vocabulary.definition: A preserve made from citrus fruit, especially oranges, usually with bits of peel.
vocabulary.example: They silently accepted the marmalade.

vocabulary.definition: Causing great wonder; extraordinarily good or wonderful.
vocabulary.example: Isn‚Äôt it marvelous!

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or expressing sadness or sorrow.
vocabulary.example: Cat had rather a melancholy time.

vocabulary.definition: Showing good manners towards others, respectful and considerate.
vocabulary.example: Roger was just being polite.

vocabulary.definition: Belonging to or for the use of one particular person or group; not public.
vocabulary.example: It was Julia and Roger‚Äôs private tree house.

vocabulary.definition: Inclined to conceal feelings and intentions or not to share information.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was being secretive.

vocabulary.definition: Without making any sound; quietly.
vocabulary.example: They silently accepted the marmalade.

vocabulary.definition: Being the only one; single.
vocabulary.example: The sole thing either of them said was...

vocabulary.definition: An area of land or water considered as a continuous expanse.
vocabulary.example: A huge stretch of turf like that.

vocabulary.definition: Causing great fear or terror.
vocabulary.example: He waited through another terrifying dinner.

vocabulary.definition: Conducive to or suggestive of good health and physical well-being.
vocabulary.example: After a wholesome lunch of turnips and boiled mutton.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magical skills, spells, and abilities associated with witches.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was doing was witchcraft.
